planting.note: Plant bare-root or container divisions in fall or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Virginia rose is extremely cold hardy and tolerates a wide range of soils, including poor, sandy, and dry conditions once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — Virginia rose is one of the most ecologically valuable of all native roses, producing simple, five-petaled, clear pink flowers in late spring with a pleasant light fragrance, followed by the best hips of any eastern wild rose: large, bright red, glossy fruits that persist on the canes through winter and provide critical food for catbirds, mockingbirds, robins, and cedar waxwings. The foliage turns brilliant shades of red, orange, and burgundy in autumn — outstanding fall color for a shrub. It spreads by suckers to form dense, wildlife-valuable thickets in naturalistic settings, along fencerows, and at the edges of woodland, and tolerates salt spray, poor sandy soils, and moderate drought. Disease resistance is excellent compared to hybrid roses: no spraying required. Prune out oldest canes after flowering to keep the plant productive; remove suckers to control spread in small spaces.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions at the water's edge or in wet, mucky soil in partial shade to full sun; yellow flag iris spreads rapidly by both rhizome and prolific seed set in wet conditions — it is listed as invasive in many US states and should not be planted near natural waterways, wetlands, or drainage areas. Deadhead promptly to prevent seed spread.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions at pond margins, in wet ditches, or in containers submerged in water gardens in full sun to partial shade — yellow flag iris is one of the most spectacularly showy wetland plants in the world, producing large, bright golden-yellow iris flowers in late spring on tall, sword-leaved clumps 3–5 feet high, thriving in standing water, stream banks, and boggy soils where few other ornamentals succeed. It is a superb water garden marginal and naturalizes rapidly in any wet, disturbed area. However, it is introduced from Europe and is listed as an invasive plant in much of the eastern and western US, crowding out native wetland vegetation — do not plant it near natural wetlands, ponds, streams, or drainage systems, and remove seed heads immediately after blooming. For water gardens, contain the rhizomes in submerged pots to prevent spread. The native blue flag iris (I. virginica) or southern blue flag (I. virginica var. shrevei) are strongly preferred alternatives in most areas.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; copper lily is native to the dry, grassy prairies and roadsides of the Gulf Coast and performs best with a summer dry period followed by rainfall to trigger flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — copper lily is a charming, small-bulbed rain lily native to the coastal prairies and grassy roadsides of southeastern Texas and Louisiana, producing star-shaped, copper-pink to rosy-pink flowers on short scapes in late summer and fall following rain events. Like all rain lilies, the flowers appear seemingly overnight after summer storms or irrigation, lasting 2–3 days before fading, with successive flushes following each rain. The narrow, rush-like leaves are nearly evergreen in zone 9–10 and die back in hard freezes. It naturalizes freely in well-drained, open soils, spreading by both offsets and seed to form dense, floriferous colonies in lawn-edge plantings, rock gardens, and sunny to lightly shaded perennial borders. An outstanding low-maintenance bulb for the Gulf Coast that requires no irrigation once established in well-adapted soils.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container specimens after last frost; downy jasmine grows rapidly in zone 9–11 and can be trained as a sprawling groundcover, a low hedge, or a soft mounding shrub depending on how it is pruned.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — downy jasmine is an Indian native jasmine widely grown along the Gulf Coast for its masses of small, starry, pure white flowers with a light, sweet fragrance produced from fall through spring during the cooler months. The soft, downy-hairy leaves and stems give it an appealing, fine-textured, gray-green appearance when out of bloom. It is a sprawling, loose shrub that can be used as a large-scale groundcover, a slope stabilizer, or a mounding informal hedge; with light pruning after bloom it stays tidy and compact. Unlike many jasmines, it tolerates partial shade and performs excellently along the shaded sides of buildings and fences. In zone 9 the foliage may drop or be damaged in hard freezes but the plant resprouts quickly from roots; in zone 10–11 it is fully evergreen. Extremely drought tolerant once established, requiring virtually no supplemental irrigation in adapted soils.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container transplants after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F; Philippine violet blooms on new growth and should be pruned back by one-third in early spring to encourage a flush of new flowering stems.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — Philippine violet is a prolific-blooming tropical shrub from South Asia that thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast, producing masses of attractive tubular, five-lobed flowers in shades of lavender-blue, violet, or white from fall through spring. It blooms most heavily during the cooler months when many other tropical plants have shut down, making it a valuable addition to the fall and winter garden palette. The soft, somewhat hairy foliage is attractive year-round in zone 10–11. It performs beautifully in partial shade and tolerates considerable drought once established — an outstanding plant for dry-shade situations along building foundations and under trees in zone 9–10. In zone 9 top growth may freeze back in hard winters but the plant regrows vigorously; cut back to 6 inches in late winter each year to encourage vigorous new blooming growth.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, neutral to alkaline, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie verbena requires excellent drainage and tolerates no standing water. Self-sows readily to naturalize across open, dry areas.

directions: Direct sow or transplant into very well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun — prairie verbena is a sprawling, finely cut-leaved native groundcover of the southern Great Plains and Gulf Coast prairies, producing clusters of bright rose-pink to lavender-pink flowers nearly year-round in warm climates from fall through spring and sporadically through summer. It is one of the most important native nectar plants for low-growing prairie butterflies including the Phaon crescent, bordered patch, and sulfur species. The silvery-green, finely dissected foliage forms an attractive, trailing mat that roots where stems contact soil, spreading to cover rocky slopes, dry banks, and open gravel. It thrives in the alkaline, caliche-heavy, or sandy soils of the southern Gulf Coast that defeat many garden plants. Essentially no supplemental irrigation is needed in adapted soils; good drainage is critical — the plant will not survive in wet or clay soils.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks and sow in spring; whorled milkweed thrives in dry, rocky, sandy, or clay soils in full sun and is one of the most drought-tolerant and widely adaptable of all native milkweeds.

directions: Direct sow in fall or transplant in spring in dry, well-drained soil in full sun — whorled milkweed is a delicate-looking but remarkably tough native milkweed of open prairies, dry roadsides, and rocky glades from Massachusetts to New Mexico, distinguished from other milkweeds by its very narrow, needle-like leaves arranged in whorls of 3–6 around slender stems. The small but numerous clusters of tiny white flowers are deliciously fragrant and are visited by dozens of native bee, butterfly, and moth species. Like all milkweeds, it is a monarch butterfly host plant — the larvae consume the foliage — and the foliage is toxic to livestock so should be avoided in pasture situations. It grows in some of the poorest soils in North America and is an ideal native groundcover for dry, sunny slopes, gravel gardens, and along dry roadsides where typical garden plants fail. An important and under-planted native milkweed for garden diversity.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; antelope horns has a large taproot that makes it drought tolerant once established but very difficult to transplant — direct sowing in its permanent location is strongly preferred.

directions: Direct sow in fall in rocky, alkaline, or sandy, well-drained, dry soil in full sun — antelope horns is the iconic native milkweed of the Texas Hill Country, southern prairies, and Chihuahuan Desert, producing clusters of unusual, intricate flowers with green-white petals reflexed back to expose greenish-maroon hoods, giving the flower cluster an otherworldly appearance. The large, broad, horizontally spreading leaves are gray-green and waxy; the paired seed pods curve upward like antelope horns, full of silky-plumed seeds in late summer. It is one of the most important early-season monarch butterfly host and nectar plants in Texas, as it emerges and blooms in spring before other milkweeds are available during the critical spring migration period. Antelope horns is a deep-rooted perennial of great drought tolerance and longevity — once established in adapted caliche, limestone gravel, or dry clay it requires virtually no care or supplemental water and lives for decades.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or plugs in spring in any well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, moist to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; frogfruit is extremely adaptable and roots readily wherever stems contact moist soil, spreading quickly to fill in bare ground.

directions: Plant plugs or rooted cuttings in any well-drained, moist to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — frogfruit is a tough, trailing native groundcover of Gulf Coast roadsides, disturbed prairies, and open woodland margins, hugging the ground so tightly (1–3 inches tall) that it can be used as a low-maintenance lawn substitute or mowed path edge in full sun. Despite its modest appearance, it is one of the most ecologically important low-growing native plants in the South: the tiny white to pink flower heads bloom nearly year-round in warm climates and serve as a critical nectar source for many small native bees and flies, while the leaves are larval host plants for the Phaon crescent and common buckeye butterflies. It roots at stem nodes wherever it contacts moist soil, spreading rapidly to colonize bare areas but never invasively outcompeting other established plants. Heat, drought, and salt tolerant; extremely low-maintenance once established. Blooms are most prolific in spring and fall.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist, loamy to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf penstemon is one of the few penstemons adapted to the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast, where most other species struggle.

directions: Direct sow in fall or transplant plugs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — Gulf penstemon is a rare native treasure: one of only a handful of Penstemon species adapted to the humid, wet winters and brutally hot summers of the Gulf Coast region from eastern Texas to Louisiana and western Mississippi. In spring it produces tall, airy spikes of small, tubular lavender to violet-blue flowers with white-striped throats that are intensely attractive to native bees (particularly bumblebees) and ruby-throated hummingbirds. It blooms prolifically in March through May, then sets seed and dies back to a basal rosette through summer heat. Self-sows reliably to persist in the garden; it thrives in moist, slightly acidic soils of pine flatwoods and stream margins — conditions very different from the dry, alkaline soils preferred by most western penstemons. The species to choose for humid South garden penstemon trials.
